#### Chatzipanagiotou et al. (2019) — Managing the Consumer-Based Brand Equity Process: A Cross-Cultural Perspective
- **[EVID-000003]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand image, brand personality, and brand associations are reported as significant predictors of attitude toward a behaviour.
- **[EVID-000004]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand awareness, trust, and perceived quality often influence perceived behavioural control and can also shape subjective norms.
- **[EVID-000005]** ✓ *moderate* — Overall brand equity has a direct positive effect on behavioural intention beyond indirect effects through IBM or TPB constructs.
- **[EVID-000007]** ✓ *moderate* — Customer satisfaction partially mediates the relationship between brand equity and behavioural outcomes such as repurchase or willingness to pay.
- **[EVID-000008]** ✓ *weak* — Brand equity builds expectations that, when met, lead to satisfaction and subsequent behaviour.
- **[EVID-000009]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity can be modelled as a configural process involving brand building, brand understanding, and brand relationship blocks.
- **[EVID-000010]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity is broadly treated as a multi-dimensional construct involving perceptual inputs, relational aspects, and quality perceptions.
- **[EVID-000011]** ✓ *weak* — Simple unidimensional measures of brand equity are insufficient for modelling behavioural effects.
- **[EVID-000012]** ✓ *moderate* — Cognitive and affective components of brand equity, especially brand image and associations, are antecedents of attitude toward the brand and related behaviours.
- **[EVID-000016]** ~ *moderate* — Cultural dimensions such as individualism and collectivism moderate the relative importance of brand equity blocks and their translation into behavioural outcomes.
- **[EVID-000017]** ~ *moderate* — Consumer price perception can moderate the relationship between brand equity dimensions and purchase intention.
- **[EVID-000018]** ~ *moderate* — Customer demographics can moderate the strength of the relationship between brand equity and behaviour.
- **[EVID-000022]** ○ *weak* — There is a need to develop and validate more standardized integrated models to improve comparability and facilitate meta-analysis across studies and sectors.
- **[EVID-000023]** ○ *weak* — The role of digital and social media platforms in shaping CBBE-IBM pathways is not fully explored, especially regarding moderation effects.
- **[EVID-000028]** ✓ *moderate* — Trust appears frequently as a core dimension of brand equity in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000098]** ✓ *strong* — Incorporating brand equity as a multi-dimensional latent construct enhances the explanatory and predictive power of IBM/TPB-based behavioural models, particularly in consumer choice contexts.
- **[EVID-000100]** ✓ *strong* — Brand equity captures cognitive drivers such as awareness and image and affective-relational drivers such as trust and loyalty.
- **[EVID-000102]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand image, brand personality, and brand associations are reported as significant predictors of attitude toward a behaviour.
- **[EVID-000104]** ✓ *strong* — Overall brand equity has a direct positive effect on behavioural intention.
- **[EVID-000106]** ✓ *moderate* — Customer satisfaction partially mediates the relationship between brand equity and behavioural outcomes such as repurchase and willingness to pay.
- **[EVID-000107]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity can be modelled as a configural process involving brand building, brand understanding, and brand relationship blocks.
- **[EVID-000109]** ✓ *strong* — Simple unidimensional measures are insufficient for representing brand equity in integrated CBBE-IBM/TPB models.
- **[EVID-000110]** ✓ *moderate* — The cognitive and affective components of brand equity, especially brand image and associations, are significant antecedents of attitude toward the brand and related behaviours.
- **[EVID-000114]** ~ *moderate* — The relative importance of brand equity blocks and their translation into behavioural outcomes is moderated by cultural dimensions such as individualism and collectivism.
- **[EVID-000115]** ~ *moderate* — Consumer price perception can moderate the relationship between brand equity dimensions and purchase intention.
- **[EVID-000116]** ~ *moderate* — Customer demographics can moderate the strength of the relationship between brand equity and behaviour.
- **[EVID-000119]** ○ *weak* — Longitudinal validation of integrated CBBE-IBM/TPB models is not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000120]** ○ *weak* — Standardized integrated models for CBBE and IBM/TPB are not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000121]** ○ *weak* — The role of digital and social media platforms in shaping CBBE-IBM pathways is not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000122]** ○ *weak* — Moderation effects in digital and social media contexts are not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000180]** ✓ *moderate* — Network and process-oriented models of brand equity are reported to offer greater explanatory adequacy than traditional static, linear indices.
- **[EVID-000187]** ✓ *moderate* — Culture is reported to moderate relationships within dynamic brand equity models.
- **[EVID-000189]** ✓ *weak* — Associative memory theory models brand equity as a network of brand knowledge nodes in the consumer’s mind.
- **[EVID-000190]** ✓ *weak* — The strength, density, and configuration of brand associations are reported to determine consumer responses beyond the mere presence of associations.
- **[EVID-000191]** ✓ *weak* — A brand stimulus is reported to activate a specific pattern within the consumer’s brand knowledge network that influences perceptions and behavior.
- **[EVID-000193]** ~ *moderate* — There is reported to be no consensus on a single universally superior brand equity model.
- **[EVID-000194]** ~ *moderate* — Systematic reviews are reported to emphasize context-specific adaptation of brand equity models by industry, culture, and research question.
- **[EVID-000198]** ○ *moderate* — A lack of standardized components and measures across process models is reported to hinder robust comparison and meta-analysis across studies, industries, and cultures.
- **[EVID-000200]** ○ *weak* — Longitudinal studies are reported to be needed to validate the predictive power of dynamic brand equity models over time and to capture the evolution of consumer-brand relationships.
- **[EVID-000717]** ✓ *weak* — In crowded fundraising environments, strong brands are proposed to help donors navigate choice complexity and reduce cognitive effort by orienting them toward familiar and trusted options.
- **[EVID-000726]** ○ *weak* — Processual or network approaches view brand equity as a dynamic network of associations.
- **[EVID-000730]** ✓ *moderate* — There is no consensus on the internal architecture of nonprofit brand equity.

#### Knowles et al. (2012) — Predictors of Young People's Charitable Intentions to Donate Money: An Extended 
- **[EVID-000277]** ✓ *strong* — Traditional models such as the Theory of Planned Behavior explain substantially less variance in actual giving behavior than in donation intentions.
- **[EVID-000281]** ✓ *strong* — Self-regulation failure prevents some individuals with positive intentions from executing donation behavior amid competing priorities.
- **[EVID-000285]** ✓ *strong* — Intention alone is an inadequate predictor of actual donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000286]** ✓ *strong* — Models of charitable giving that terminate at intention formation are insufficient for explaining actual giving behavior.
- **[EVID-000291]** ✓ *strong* — The Theory of Planned Behavior is reported as a robust and widely validated framework for predicting charitable donation intentions across contexts including monetary giving, blood or organ donation, 
- **[EVID-000298]** ✓ *moderate* — Past behavior can directly influence both donation intention and future donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000303]** ~ *moderate* — The relative predictive weight of Theory of Planned Behavior constructs varies across charitable giving contexts.
- **[EVID-000312]** ○ *weak* — There is a lack of research testing and adapting the Theory of Planned Behavior model in diverse non-Western cultural settings.
- **[EVID-000321]** ✓ *moderate* — Past donation behavior is reported as a significant predictor of future donation intentions.
- **[EVID-000322]** ✓ *moderate* — Past donation behavior is reported as indicating the role of habit and experience in charitable giving.
- **[EVID-000381]** ✓ *moderate* — Awareness, associations or image, perceived quality, and loyalty are consistently identified as core dimensions of brand equity across sectors.
- **[EVID-000382]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity is widely modeled as a multi-level or sequential process from foundational perceptions to relational outcomes.
- **[EVID-000383]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity concepts are adaptable from commercial contexts to nonprofit, social, and employer branding settings.
- **[EVID-000384]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity constructs function as antecedents to Theory of Planned Behavior components and thereby influence behavioral intentions.
- **[EVID-000386]** ✓ *moderate* — A strong and positive brand image fosters a more favorable attitude, which is a primary driver of intention in TPB and IBM-type models.
- **[EVID-000391]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity develops in stages from basic awareness to deeper relational commitment.
- **[EVID-000393]** ✓ *strong* — Extended Theory of Planned Behavior models are the prevailing framework for explaining charitable donation intentions and behavior in the reviewed donation literature.
- **[EVID-000394]** ✓ *strong* — Moral norms and perceived behavioral control often emerge as stronger predictors of charitable donation intention than attitude toward the charitable organization.
- **[EVID-000395]** ✓ *strong* — Meta-analytic evidence confirms that moral norms and perceived behavioral control are consistently strong predictors of charitable giving intentions.
- **[EVID-000396]** ✗ *moderate* — Some charitable giving studies found that attitude toward the specific charitable organization was not a significant predictor of donation intention.
- **[EVID-000397]** ~ *moderate* — Moral norms, past behavior, and perceived behavioral control were dominant predictors of donation intention in studies where attitude toward the charitable organization was nonsignificant.
- **[EVID-000398]** ✗ *moderate* — Some studies found subjective and descriptive norms to be weak or nonsignificant predictors of donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000399]** ✓ *moderate* — Other studies found social norms to be influential in donation contexts when connected to contextual factors such as reputation or religiosity.
- **[EVID-000400]** ~ *strong* — A systematic review found that social information can have positive, null, or negative effects on donation amounts.
- **[EVID-000402]** ~ *moderate* — The specific drivers and hierarchical structure of brand equity differ by context, such that a retailer equity model differs from an NGO donor-based equity model.
- **[EVID-000404]** ✓ *moderate* — Organizational reputation can moderate the predictive power of behavioral models for donation intention.
- **[EVID-000406]** ✓ *moderate* — Individual involvement moderates the effectiveness of brand-related communications.
- **[EVID-000407]** ✓ *moderate* — Norm-based messaging was more effective for highly involved individuals.
- **[EVID-000409]** ✓ *moderate* — The reviewed literature provides evidence of compatibility and overlap between brand equity frameworks and TPB or IBM-like behavioral models.
- **[EVID-000717]** ✓ *weak* — In crowded fundraising environments, strong brands are proposed to help donors navigate choice complexity and reduce cognitive effort by orienting them toward familiar and trusted options.

- **[EVID-001884]** ✓ *moderate* — Higher-order brand associations like reputation, differentiation, and identification are more significant drivers of donor-based brand equity than simple brand familiarity.
- **[EVID-001888]** ✗ *moderate* — Existing nonprofit brand equity indices and models have limited cross-cultural robustness, often being developed and validated in specific national contexts.
- **[EVID-001894]** ~ *moderate* — Brand equity models are not easily transferable across different nonprofit stakeholder groups without adaptation.
- **[EVID-001895]** ✓ *strong* — Brand equity scales developed for the commercial sector are not directly applicable to nonprofits and require significant adaptation.
- **[EVID-001918]** ✓ *moderate* — Nonprofit brands can act as cognitive heuristics or cues that reduce donor uncertainty.
- **[EVID-001929]** ✓ *strong* — The Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) is a dominant and empirically robust framework for explaining charitable donation intention.
- **[EVID-001933]** ○ *strong* — There is no established, empirically tested model that integrates donor-based brand equity directly into a Theory of Planned Behavior or Integrated Behavioral Model framework for giving.
- **[EVID-002064]** ✓ *strong* — Charitable giving is driven by a mix of motives, including altruism, egoism (warm glow), values, trust, and social context.
- **[EVID-002066]** ✓ *strong* — Donors derive private, emotional utility, known as "warm glow," from the act of giving itself, independent of the actual outcome.

#### Fehresti, S., Takian, A., Jaafaripooyan, E., Parsaeian, M., & Jalilian, H. (2021) — Predicting donors’ behavior in contributing to health compared to non-health sec
- **[EVID-000254]** ✓ *strong* — Attitude, perceived norms, and perceived behavioural control are consistent and significant predictors of intention to donate.
- **[EVID-000255]** ✓ *moderate* — Intention is consistently reported as the strongest single predictor of actual donation behaviour.
- **[EVID-000256]** ~ *moderate* — The relationship between donation intention and actual donation behaviour is positive and significant but incomplete.
- **[EVID-000263]** ~ *moderate* — Standard IBM or TPB predictors may be less powerful in non-Western contexts where macro-level factors such as income, institutional trust, or happiness can be stronger predictors of giving.
- **[EVID-000267]** ✓ *moderate* — In digital donation contexts, trust in the technological platform is necessary in addition to trust in the beneficiary organization.
- **[EVID-000273]** ○ *weak* — There is a deficit of studies applying and testing intention-based donation models in diverse non-Western contexts.
- **[EVID-000277]** ✓ *strong* — Traditional models such as the Theory of Planned Behavior explain substantially less variance in actual giving behavior than in donation intentions.
- **[EVID-000281]** ✓ *strong* — Self-regulation failure prevents some individuals with positive intentions from executing donation behavior amid competing priorities.
- **[EVID-000285]** ✓ *strong* — Intention alone is an inadequate predictor of actual donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000286]** ✓ *strong* — Models of charitable giving that terminate at intention formation are insufficient for explaining actual giving behavior.
- **[EVID-000291]** ✓ *strong* — The Theory of Planned Behavior is reported as a robust and widely validated framework for predicting charitable donation intentions across contexts including monetary giving, blood or organ donation, 
- **[EVID-000298]** ✓ *moderate* — Past behavior can directly influence both donation intention and future donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000303]** ~ *moderate* — The relative predictive weight of Theory of Planned Behavior constructs varies across charitable giving contexts.
- **[EVID-000312]** ○ *weak* — There is a lack of research testing and adapting the Theory of Planned Behavior model in diverse non-Western cultural settings.
- **[EVID-001857]** ✓ *strong* — The standard Theory of Planned Behavior explains 44% of the variance in charitable donation intention and 19% of the variance in actual donation behavior.
- **[EVID-001858]** ✓ *strong* — Extending the standard Theory of Planned Behavior with moral norms and past behavior significantly improves its explanatory power for donation intention.
- **[EVID-001859]** ✓ *strong* — Adding moral norms to the standard TPB model increased the explained variance in donation intention from 44% to 52% in a meta-analysis.
- **[EVID-001860]** ✓ *strong* — Personal moral norms are a powerful and significant predictor of charitable giving intention, often adding more explanatory power than standard TPB variables.
- **[EVID-001861]** ✓ *strong* — Past donation behavior is a strong and consistent predictor of future donation intention.
- **[EVID-001862]** ✗ *strong* — A significant intention-behavior gap exists in charitable giving, where models explain intention substantially better than they explain actual behavior.
- **[EVID-001863]** ✓ *moderate* — Injunctive social norms, reflecting perceived social approval, are a more consistent and significant predictor of donation intention than descriptive norms.
- **[EVID-001864]** ~ *moderate* — The effect of descriptive social norms, reflecting the perceived behavior of others, on donation intention is inconsistent across studies.
- **[EVID-001865]** ✓ *moderate* — Social norms can moderate the link between donation intention and subsequent donation behavior.
- **[EVID-001866]** ~ *strong* — The predictive strength of different Theory of Planned Behavior components varies depending on the type of charitable act being studied.
- **[EVID-001867]** ○ *moderate* — The reviewed literature lacks a direct, formal comparison of a distinct Integrated Behavioral Model (IBM) against an extended TPB in the context of general charitable giving.
- **[EVID-001868]** ○ *moderate* — The specific role of donor or role identity as a moderator of the intention-behavior relationship is not directly tested in the cited literature.

#### Chatzipanagiotou et al. (2025) — Managing Brand Assets Internally: Turning Employees Into an Integral Source of B
- **[EVID-000003]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand image, brand personality, and brand associations are reported as significant predictors of attitude toward a behaviour.
- **[EVID-000004]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand awareness, trust, and perceived quality often influence perceived behavioural control and can also shape subjective norms.
- **[EVID-000005]** ✓ *moderate* — Overall brand equity has a direct positive effect on behavioural intention beyond indirect effects through IBM or TPB constructs.
- **[EVID-000007]** ✓ *moderate* — Customer satisfaction partially mediates the relationship between brand equity and behavioural outcomes such as repurchase or willingness to pay.
- **[EVID-000008]** ✓ *weak* — Brand equity builds expectations that, when met, lead to satisfaction and subsequent behaviour.
- **[EVID-000009]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity can be modelled as a configural process involving brand building, brand understanding, and brand relationship blocks.
- **[EVID-000010]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity is broadly treated as a multi-dimensional construct involving perceptual inputs, relational aspects, and quality perceptions.
- **[EVID-000011]** ✓ *weak* — Simple unidimensional measures of brand equity are insufficient for modelling behavioural effects.
- **[EVID-000012]** ✓ *moderate* — Cognitive and affective components of brand equity, especially brand image and associations, are antecedents of attitude toward the brand and related behaviours.
- **[EVID-000016]** ~ *moderate* — Cultural dimensions such as individualism and collectivism moderate the relative importance of brand equity blocks and their translation into behavioural outcomes.
- **[EVID-000017]** ~ *moderate* — Consumer price perception can moderate the relationship between brand equity dimensions and purchase intention.
- **[EVID-000018]** ~ *moderate* — Customer demographics can moderate the strength of the relationship between brand equity and behaviour.
- **[EVID-000022]** ○ *weak* — There is a need to develop and validate more standardized integrated models to improve comparability and facilitate meta-analysis across studies and sectors.
- **[EVID-000023]** ○ *weak* — The role of digital and social media platforms in shaping CBBE-IBM pathways is not fully explored, especially regarding moderation effects.
- **[EVID-000028]** ✓ *moderate* — Trust appears frequently as a core dimension of brand equity in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000098]** ✓ *strong* — Incorporating brand equity as a multi-dimensional latent construct enhances the explanatory and predictive power of IBM/TPB-based behavioural models, particularly in consumer choice contexts.
- **[EVID-000100]** ✓ *strong* — Brand equity captures cognitive drivers such as awareness and image and affective-relational drivers such as trust and loyalty.
- **[EVID-000102]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand image, brand personality, and brand associations are reported as significant predictors of attitude toward a behaviour.
- **[EVID-000104]** ✓ *strong* — Overall brand equity has a direct positive effect on behavioural intention.
- **[EVID-000106]** ✓ *moderate* — Customer satisfaction partially mediates the relationship between brand equity and behavioural outcomes such as repurchase and willingness to pay.
- **[EVID-000107]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity can be modelled as a configural process involving brand building, brand understanding, and brand relationship blocks.
- **[EVID-000109]** ✓ *strong* — Simple unidimensional measures are insufficient for representing brand equity in integrated CBBE-IBM/TPB models.
- **[EVID-000110]** ✓ *moderate* — The cognitive and affective components of brand equity, especially brand image and associations, are significant antecedents of attitude toward the brand and related behaviours.
- **[EVID-000114]** ~ *moderate* — The relative importance of brand equity blocks and their translation into behavioural outcomes is moderated by cultural dimensions such as individualism and collectivism.
- **[EVID-000115]** ~ *moderate* — Consumer price perception can moderate the relationship between brand equity dimensions and purchase intention.
- **[EVID-000116]** ~ *moderate* — Customer demographics can moderate the strength of the relationship between brand equity and behaviour.
- **[EVID-000119]** ○ *weak* — Longitudinal validation of integrated CBBE-IBM/TPB models is not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000120]** ○ *weak* — Standardized integrated models for CBBE and IBM/TPB are not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000121]** ○ *weak* — The role of digital and social media platforms in shaping CBBE-IBM pathways is not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000122]** ○ *weak* — Moderation effects in digital and social media contexts are not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-000180]** ✓ *moderate* — Network and process-oriented models of brand equity are reported to offer greater explanatory adequacy than traditional static, linear indices.
- **[EVID-000187]** ✓ *moderate* — Culture is reported to moderate relationships within dynamic brand equity models.
- **[EVID-000189]** ✓ *weak* — Associative memory theory models brand equity as a network of brand knowledge nodes in the consumer’s mind.
- **[EVID-000190]** ✓ *weak* — The strength, density, and configuration of brand associations are reported to determine consumer responses beyond the mere presence of associations.
- **[EVID-000191]** ✓ *weak* — A brand stimulus is reported to activate a specific pattern within the consumer’s brand knowledge network that influences perceptions and behavior.
- **[EVID-000193]** ~ *moderate* — There is reported to be no consensus on a single universally superior brand equity model.
- **[EVID-000194]** ~ *moderate* — Systematic reviews are reported to emphasize context-specific adaptation of brand equity models by industry, culture, and research question.
- **[EVID-000198]** ○ *moderate* — A lack of standardized components and measures across process models is reported to hinder robust comparison and meta-analysis across studies, industries, and cultures.
- **[EVID-000200]** ○ *weak* — Longitudinal studies are reported to be needed to validate the predictive power of dynamic brand equity models over time and to capture the evolution of consumer-brand relationships.

- **[EVID-000347]** ✓ *weak* — The reviewed literature reports that brand-like cues can reduce perceived risk associated with donation.
- **[EVID-000717]** ✓ *weak* — In crowded fundraising environments, strong brands are proposed to help donors navigate choice complexity and reduce cognitive effort by orienting them toward familiar and trusted options.
- **[EVID-001902]** ✓ *strong* — The Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) is a foundational and highly predictive model for charitable donation intention.
- **[EVID-001903]** ✓ *strong* — Perceived Behavioral Control (PBC) is the strongest predictor within the Theory of Planned Behavior for donation intention.
- **[EVID-001904]** ✓ *strong* — Adding moral norms to the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) significantly increases its explanatory power for donation intention.
- **[EVID-001905]** ✓ *moderate* — Donors use organizational trust and reputation as proxy signals to reduce the uncertainty inherent in evaluating nonprofits as credence goods.
- **[EVID-001906]** ✓ *strong* — Trust in a nonprofit organization is a significant positive predictor of charitable giving intentions and behavior.
- **[EVID-001907]** ✓ *moderate* — Organizational reputation influences donation intention through both a direct path and an indirect path mediated by organizational trust.
- **[EVID-001908]** ✓ *moderate* — A donor's identification with a nonprofit's brand is a direct predictor of their intention to donate.
- **[EVID-001909]** ✓ *moderate* — Past donation behaviour is a strong predictor of future donation intention.
- **[EVID-001910]** ✓ *strong* — Fundraising appeals framed around donor-centric benefits like warm glow are more effective at increasing donation likelihood and amount than recipient-centric appeals.
- **[EVID-001911]** ~ *moderate* — The direct effect of dispositional traits such as altruism and self-esteem on donation intention is inconsistent across studies.
- **[EVID-001912]** ~ *moderate* — Warm-glow motivation is an important driver of donations but is not a sufficient explanation on its own, operating alongside other motives like altruism and trust.
- **[EVID-001913]** ✓ *moderate* — The psychological drivers for donating time are systematically different from the drivers for donating money.
- **[EVID-001915]** ✓ *moderate* — The dimensions of perceived organizational trustworthiness (ability, integrity, and benevolence) positively influence donation attitudes or intention.
- **[EVID-001916]** ○ *weak* — Donor satisfaction as a predictor of donor loyalty and repeat giving behaviour is not sufficiently covered in the reviewed literature.
- **[EVID-001929]** ✓ *strong* — The Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) is a dominant and empirically robust framework for explaining charitable donation intention.
- **[EVID-001933]** ○ *strong* — There is no established, empirically tested model that integrates donor-based brand equity directly into a Theory of Planned Behavior or Integrated Behavioral Model framework for giving.

#### Anggraeni and Kuklienė (2024) — Multidimensional Analysis of Charitable Behavior: Integration of Altruism, Relig
- **[EVID-000254]** ✓ *strong* — Attitude, perceived norms, and perceived behavioural control are consistent and significant predictors of intention to donate.
- **[EVID-000255]** ✓ *moderate* — Intention is consistently reported as the strongest single predictor of actual donation behaviour.
- **[EVID-000256]** ~ *moderate* — The relationship between donation intention and actual donation behaviour is positive and significant but incomplete.
- **[EVID-000263]** ~ *moderate* — Standard IBM or TPB predictors may be less powerful in non-Western contexts where macro-level factors such as income, institutional trust, or happiness can be stronger predictors of giving.
- **[EVID-000267]** ✓ *moderate* — In digital donation contexts, trust in the technological platform is necessary in addition to trust in the beneficiary organization.
- **[EVID-000273]** ○ *weak* — There is a deficit of studies applying and testing intention-based donation models in diverse non-Western contexts.
- **[EVID-000291]** ✓ *strong* — The Theory of Planned Behavior is reported as a robust and widely validated framework for predicting charitable donation intentions across contexts including monetary giving, blood or organ donation, 
- **[EVID-000298]** ✓ *moderate* — Past behavior can directly influence both donation intention and future donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000303]** ~ *moderate* — The relative predictive weight of Theory of Planned Behavior constructs varies across charitable giving contexts.
- **[EVID-000312]** ○ *weak* — There is a lack of research testing and adapting the Theory of Planned Behavior model in diverse non-Western cultural settings.
- **[EVID-000381]** ✓ *moderate* — Awareness, associations or image, perceived quality, and loyalty are consistently identified as core dimensions of brand equity across sectors.
- **[EVID-000382]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity is widely modeled as a multi-level or sequential process from foundational perceptions to relational outcomes.
- **[EVID-000383]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity concepts are adaptable from commercial contexts to nonprofit, social, and employer branding settings.
- **[EVID-000384]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity constructs function as antecedents to Theory of Planned Behavior components and thereby influence behavioral intentions.
- **[EVID-000386]** ✓ *moderate* — A strong and positive brand image fosters a more favorable attitude, which is a primary driver of intention in TPB and IBM-type models.
- **[EVID-000391]** ✓ *moderate* — Brand equity develops in stages from basic awareness to deeper relational commitment.
- **[EVID-000393]** ✓ *strong* — Extended Theory of Planned Behavior models are the prevailing framework for explaining charitable donation intentions and behavior in the reviewed donation literature.
- **[EVID-000394]** ✓ *strong* — Moral norms and perceived behavioral control often emerge as stronger predictors of charitable donation intention than attitude toward the charitable organization.
- **[EVID-000395]** ✓ *strong* — Meta-analytic evidence confirms that moral norms and perceived behavioral control are consistently strong predictors of charitable giving intentions.
- **[EVID-000396]** ✗ *moderate* — Some charitable giving studies found that attitude toward the specific charitable organization was not a significant predictor of donation intention.
- **[EVID-000397]** ~ *moderate* — Moral norms, past behavior, and perceived behavioral control were dominant predictors of donation intention in studies where attitude toward the charitable organization was nonsignificant.
- **[EVID-000398]** ✗ *moderate* — Some studies found subjective and descriptive norms to be weak or nonsignificant predictors of donation behavior.
- **[EVID-000399]** ✓ *moderate* — Other studies found social norms to be influential in donation contexts when connected to contextual factors such as reputation or religiosity.
- **[EVID-000399]** ✓ *moderate* — Other studies found social norms to be influential in donation contexts when connected to contextual factors such as reputation or religiosity.
- **[EVID-000400]** ~ *strong* — A systematic review found that social information can have positive, null, or negative effects on donation amounts.
- **[EVID-000402]** ~ *moderate* — The specific drivers and hierarchical structure of brand equity differ by context, such that a retailer equity model differs from an NGO donor-based equity model.
- **[EVID-000404]** ✓ *moderate* — Organizational reputation can moderate the predictive power of behavioral models for donation intention.
- **[EVID-000406]** ✓ *moderate* — Individual involvement moderates the effectiveness of brand-related communications.
- **[EVID-000407]** ✓ *moderate* — Norm-based messaging was more effective for highly involved individuals.
- **[EVID-000409]** ✓ *moderate* — The reviewed literature provides evidence of compatibility and overlap between brand equity frameworks and TPB or IBM-like behavioral models.

*… 258 additional works omitted for brevity. See Reference List for full source inventory.*
